CURRENT AFFAIRS.

Certainly the West Belfast Election is being worked
not only for all it is worth, but for a great deal more.
Even Mr. John Dillon, who never raised his voice in
the cause of Justice, Toleration and Fair Play on the
floor of the Board Room of the Great Northern bigot
railway, must have a suspicion that the thing is being
miles over done. There was yet another banquet to the
member for West Belfast, and one of the flamboyant
headings to_the report of it in the Simpiy Deplorable
was “The Union of Orange and Green.”” Mr. Michael
Davitt, whose public reputation has been so badly shat-
‘tered by his recent muddled excursion into an educa-
tional controversy, was the principal speaker. Ho was
in the prophetic vein. “The victory of West Belfast,

said he, “was the herald of the approaching triumphs
of Home Rule.’”” Wo have heard these sort of tales
- before, and we are a bit weary of them.

Mr. Michael Davitt was very anxious to “ prove his
tolerance ’’' ;: — i

,-Thu’yv are for religious freedom and not for scctarian ascondancy
(applause).  And thoy know, or will know, that we who are

Catholics seok no such supremacy in any way or sense, that we
covot nono of tho property or wealth of other Faiths, and that
Homo Rule, instead of being in any sense “ Rome Rule,” will be
tho rulo of laymen, who will be only Irishmen in National politics
(cheors), whother Catholic or Protestant in religion, and who will
brook no intervention of any kind in the domain of public life
from any scct or church or creed, Catholic or Protestant which
attempts to infringe or invads to the extent of the smallest item
of porsonal or National liberty, the religious or civil or political
freedom of this country and its people (loud cheers).

Why all this protestation? Were the Irish Catholics
ever intolerant? Why, Mr. Dillon is so ‘ tolerant ™
that he never lifted his voice on the floor of the Board
room of the Great Northern Bigot Railway.

A few years ago we found that Mr. John Dillon’s
name in the G. N.. Railway Shareholders’ List was
starred. That meant that he had a holding sufficient
to qualify him for Directorship. - But Mr. Dillon, the
friend of the Boers, of oppressed humanity in general,
never lifted his voice on the floor of the Board Room of
the Great Northern Railway. In the current Share-
holders’ List of the G. N. Railway, we find that Mr.
Dillon’s name is still there but the star is gone; he has
cevidently unloaded somewhat, and though still a share-
holder, his holding is no longer large enough to qualify
him for Directorship. Perhaps the champion of op-
pressed peoples all over the world was afraid that some-
oune might propose him as a Director on the Great
Northern Railway! At this dinner, at which Mr.
Davitt was the principal spokesman, Mr. Dillon said : —
“ We are assembled to celebrate the awakening of the
Protestant democracy of Ulster.”” Mr. Dillon went
on :—“T would say to all of you to fix your minds upon
this fact that the main weapon and instrument by
which England fixed her rule and blight and her ascen-
dancy upon this country has been by religious dis-
cord. Had it not been for that we should have had a
free Ireland long ago (hear, hear).” Well, we would say
to all our readers to fix their minds on this fact—that
Ireland would probably have had an easy time if the
vatives had accepted the English “ Reformation,”” had
they taken Archbishop Whately to their bosoms, had
they stoned the priests and cursed the Pope. We would
point out this to our readers, that when the waters of
Anglicisation broke through the barriers of language
that the distinct identity of the race was still to a great.
extent effectively preserved, and the full force of the
Anglicising flood impeded, by another defence, by the
Catholic Faith of the people. Had Ireland avoided
“ religious “discord ”’ by caving in to the Soupers as
she might have done, it would no doubt have been better
for the stomachs of the peoplo; they would have been
welcomed even into the service of the Great Northern
Railway.

Mr. Dillon further said:—* When I am asked to
take up the Catholic cause, my answer is that I am
fighting for the greatest of Catholic causes when I am
fighting for Ireland, and to give fair play to all religions
in a free Ireland (cheers), and, therefore, again I con-
gratulate the Nationalists of Belfast.”’ The people are
not such fools now as to take that for an excuse for
refusing to lift even a voice in the Board room of the
Great Northern Railway in the cause of Tolerance, Jus-
tico and Fair Play. Why does he not fight for fair
play to all religions in the Great Northern Railway as
he has the responsibilitics of part proprietorship of that
property on his shoulders? -

The growing popularity of the motor car is responsible
for a rapidly increasing industry, of which we are glad
to see some Irish firms aro determined to have a share.
We learn that the Burke Engineering and Motor Com-

Pany, which has recently been established in Clonmel,
is_about bringing out a new automobile called the
 Burke motor.”” For the present the parts of this
car are being made by various engincering firms from
the design of the Burke Company, in whose works they
aro subsequently assembled into the complote article.
Wo see, however, by an account furnished by the Clon-
mel firm that it is not their intention to stop here, but
that they intend, on the completion of their factory, to
build their car throughout even to the smallest details.
In Dublin we notice that Mr. J. J. Keating, who is
nothing if not enterprising, has turned ono of his
premises in Abbey Street into a special Depét for the
sale of motor cars and the execution of all kinds of
repairs in connection with them. As motor cars, like
watches, occasionally go wrong, it strikes us that the
repairs branch of the business alone is well worth con-
sidering as an industrial asset. We also notice that
two of the Ducros family have come back to Ireland and
widened the field of employment in Dublin in this line.

As may be seen elsewhere in our columns, Mr, T. J.
Loughlin announces that his premises in Parliament
Street will be closed on the National Holiday. Mr.
Loughlin also announces that he will, as has been his
custom for years past, hand over to the Language Fund
10 per cent. of his sales of Irish poplin neckwear during
the month of March. So that Irish Irelanders pur-
chasing from him in this line in the present month will
have the double satisfaction of supporting Irish manu-
facture and the Language cause.

The Roman correspondent of the Catholic Weekly,
:—"“ The University of Louvain has conferred the
ee of Doctor in Philosophy, Honoris causa, on the
Very Rev. Dr. O’Riordan, Rector of the Irish College.
6 is the second time since the Institute of Higher
Philosophy was founded there that the University has
conferred the honour. I understand that the honour
has no connection with his appointment to the Rector-
ship of the Irish College. I have it on the best
authority that it was notified to him during last sum-
mer before he had any thought of being Rector. But
he kept it to himself, as he also did the conferring of
the degree, which was made last November after the
opening of the first University term. But he was well-
known in Louvain, and especially to the new Arch-
bishop of Mechlin, Mgr. Mercier, till now one of the
leading members of the university, and his work,
¢ Catholicity and Progress in Ireland,’ is read and ad-
mired at the University, and has been very favourably
criticised in one of the University reviews.””

There is a discussion concerning “ Christian Reunion’’
troubling the minds of the “ Saved ”’ in Ircland. Pas-
tor Brown, we are told, has stated the Baptist case, so
we assume that Pastor Brown is a man of light and
leading amongst his particular sect. We have read
Pastor Brown’s rather lengthy remarks concerning
“ Christian Reunion.”” Into mabters of theology we, as
we are not theologians, do not enter, and we look at
this article by Pastor Brown from the point of view of
the lay “Idolator ’ in the street, who casually hears
men talking by his side about how to ““ save ”’ him from
“Idolatry.”

This mild and benevolent Baptist Pastor—we are
sure he is both the one and the other—having discussed
some bearings of Home Rule on his subject, states:—

However theso issues may shape themselves it is obviously of
supreme moment that Protestants, and, above all, Christians,
should be alive to tho importance of tho prosent crisis, und s
concentrato and unite their forces wherover possible, ag to meot
vith an unbroken front as far as practical the common foo af
civil and religious liborty—Romanism; and also to present fo
sockers for light and freedom among that system tho most attrac.
tive phaso and form of that gospol and ‘““faith which wue onco
for all delivered unto the saints.”

“«

As being of the “common foé of civil and x'oligi;;us

liberty ”’—for short, “Idolators”’—we are naturally
interested in this suggested combination against ob-
scurantists, including our unworthy selyes.

Pastor then sets oub the outlines in three pz\rfmgrap!]?‘

in addition, if we would reach Irishmen, we must do so

our race,

And,

Until our people see that our Chur ¢ native born, native
manned, and native in their eympath we cannot expect to
mako much headway in our day and generation.

So we are to be snatched from the burning! We are
not Christians at all, we are merely people belonging to
“the common foe of civil and religious liberty—
Romanism.”” We ought to feel very humble and peni-
tent standing in the dread presence of Pastor Brown—
but somehow or other, so perverse is human nature, we
feel like brazening the thing out and dying game. If
Pastor Brown should shout for the Irish Revival some
of these fine days, we may not unreasonably suspzct that
it is our “ Idolatrous’’ souls that he has an eye after
rather than the Irish nation; for he has told us that
“Protestants, and, above all, Christians,”” should unite
their forces wherever possible so as to meet with un-
broken front “Romanism.” It would be bigotry and
“ sectarianism ’’ on our part if we were to suggest that
Pastor Brown and his like might be ter employed
uniting their forces, say, in the Potteries in *“Saved ”
England.

All the way from N. Dak., U.S.A., we have reccived
a cutting from an English paper, the Daily Mirror, of
December 6th, last. The cutting contains an advertise-
ment of a London firm of tailors who supply suits 7o
measure at 21s. and 27s 6d., and pairs of trouser
6s. and 8s. The advertisement contains four te
monials. One is headed, “ A Scotsman knows a good
thing when he sees it.”’ Another, “ An Englishman
kuows when he has made a good bargain;’’ a third, “A
Londoner knows how to save money;’’ and the fourth,
““An Trishman knows whether he has received full
value.”” We were interested in the testimonial from
Treland. It is from one J. Bolger, Park Fouse,
IMarold’s Cross, Dublin. Mr. Bolger, of Harold’s
Cross, writes:—“I received your suit, and 1
must say that I am delighted with it. It fits per-
fectly, and looks equal to a three-guinea suit. I showed
it to some of my friends, and they were amazed at the
price. Please send me some more measurement forms,
and also more patterns of a different variety.”” Now that
the printers of Dublin have formed an employment com-
mittee to look after their interests, might not the
tailors do likewise?

When may we expect the addresses to Lord Aberdeen
to cease? They are a weariness to the eye in the news
columns of the daily papers. Does anyone read them?
Woe don’t.

We have not yet received a list of the Nutting
Scholars, who are also “Idolators.”” Wo hear that
there are some ““ Idolatrous *’ girls attending the Soupar
Universiby also. Perhaps some of our readers could
send us a list of them for publication.

The Catholic Young Man for March is an excep-
tionally good number. The Editorial Notes are fresh
and interesting, and there are several good articles.
Mr, J., J. Horgan’s subject for the fifth of his interesting
series of “Catholic Forces’’ is Mr. Edward Martyn.
By the way, Mr. IHorgan does not mention the fact

that Mr, Martyn was at one time President of the

Catholic Association.  An interesting ‘ Letter on
Books *’ deals chiefly with Fathor Guinan’s “Soggarth
Aroon,” and with “Tom O’Kelly.”” In the course of

The March issue of the New Ireland Review is an all
round good number. The first article, “ Where does
Irish History Begin?” is by Mr. John MacNeill. Mr.
James Fitzgerald-Kenney adds a contribution to the
controversy that has sprung up around Mr. Michael
Davitt’s simply deplorable views on Education. Father
George O’Neill, S.J., writes a very readable article on
“ Recent Irish Drama and Its Critics,”’ and Mr. Arthur
Clery, K.C., adds another chapter to his  Early Irish
History.

Whilst banquets of Nationalists ring with half-slave
“ tolerance proving,” we see that Sloan, who ratted,
was entertained to dinner by the Protestant Alliance!
According to the report in the Simply Deplorable, Sloan
said :—“ Home Rule was Rome Rule, and that was
one reason why he was so strongly opposed to it.”
That’s all the Dillons, Davitts, and Devlins get for
their loud-mouthed tolerance proving’’ and raimeis
about West Belfast. If they want to appease the Pro-
testant Alliance and the Orangemen we fear they will
have to hecome “jumpers!”’ So long as they retain
their connection with “the Italian Mission ’ we fear
they will remain suspect in the eyes of Sandy Row.

Here is a pretty Bung story. At the Coolderry
Petty Sessions, Quinn, a drunkery owner of Athleague,
was charged with selling drink to a child named Patrick
Ward under 14 years of age on two occasions.  The
drunkery owner was also charged with permitting the
boy to be intoxicated on his premises. The child Ward
it appears is a carter in the employ of one Mr. A.
Kenny, J.P. He, the child Ward, went to Roscommon
for some goods. It was alleged that he got drink at
Quinn’s on the way out and again on the way back,
anyway when he arrived home to Ballygar in the even-
ingy the child carter was very drunk. According to
the evidence of a fellow carter, one Connolly—a man
this time—Connolly and the child went into Quinn’s
public-house in Athleague, where “a quart’” was
ordered and the child had a glass of it. At Roscommon
the child had a further glass of porter and some bis-
cuits.  On the way back the child was served with
another glass at Quinn’s at Athleague. A local news-
paper report states that this man gave his evidence with
evident reluctance. The man Connolly on the way
home went back part of the road in consequence of
something he had heard. He found the child Ward
sitfing on the road! He pubt him into his cart, but
the infant was too drunk to sit up, so fearing the boy
would fall out of the cart, Connolly deposed that he
made him walk home! The child’s mother stated that
he was 13 years of age and a certificate to that effect
wag put in. When the oath was being administered to

the child in Court, the infant began to cry, and said
that he did not know what the naturo of an oath was!
Evidently ho knew the nature of Mr. Bung's porter
better. Mrs. Quinn was fined £1 in each of the three
charges. Two other Bungs were proceeded against in
connection with the samo child, but the cases against
them were dismissed. We assume that Quinn’s
drunkery will be open—to oblige the public of course—
on S8t. Patrick’s Day. The owner of Quinn’s of A.th-
league, should at once be made an honorary some'(;hmg
or other of the Bung’s Protection Society of Dublin, of
which Mr. Robert Russell, a man of sentiment, s
Secrotary.

At a recent meeting of the Corporation of Limerick,
it was resolved by 14 votes to 6, that no advertising
contract be given to a newspaper not printed on Irish-
made paper.

The forthcoming Multyfarnham Bazaar will be a
good advertisement for Irish industries. Multyfarn-
ham Abbey is 600 years old, and it is proposed to honour
ils sixth centenary by restoring both it and the church.
The encouragement of Irish manufacture is one of the
lcading features of the bazaar. His Holiness the Pope,
Cardinal Vannutelli, and others send some prizes, and
these naturally enough are not prizes of Irish manufac-
ture, but the promoters of the bazaar are sparing no
efforts to make it a bazaar, as far as possible, associated
with Irish manufacture. We have before us a list of
two hundred prizes. The names of the makers of the
various Irish articles are given so that people may be
at they are genuine. We note that the third
prize is a Pierce cycle, presented by the Archbishop of
New York; the fourth a silver tea-pot made by Gun-
ning and Reynolds of Dublin, and presented by the
Archbishop of Trinidad. To mention a few more of the
prizes—there is a water-proof rug made by Crotty and
Co. ; silk umbrella by E. Smyth and Co., Dublin; Fox-
ford tweed; Tara brooch by Hopkins and Hopkins;
Irish poplin by Elliott; set of poplin vestments made
by Miss Cahill, Dublin ; double-barrelled gun by Mr. L.
Keegan ; a Pierce plow ; meerschaum pipe by Kapp and
Peterson ; circular mirror by the Youghal Art Metal
Works; Limerick ham (O’Mara); box of Irish-made
cigars; bicycle saddle by Duffy and Co. of Carrickma-
cross, and so on, and so on. The bazaar tickets are
printed on Irish paper with Harrington’s Irish-made
ink. This genuine and thorough-going attempt to as-
sociate the bazaar with tbe Industrial Revival will, we
are sure, gain for it the special good-will of our readers
at home and abroad.

The articles by “ An Insurance Manager’’ that, as
a result of our article on the question in our issue of
January 27th, have recently appeared in the LEADER,
have excited a great deal of interest. Several commu-

_nications have reached this office for our contributor in

reply to his request contained in his article last week.
Woe have every reason to hope that something may ke
dond to save Ireland from the reproach that she has
to rely on outsiders to do her insurance work.

The citizens of Cork are at present very much in-
tevestod, if not excited, over “the Shrubberies,’” what-
over they are. The original, and we should say the
orammatical, name of the park in which the “Inter-
national  Shows at Cork were held was “the Shrub-
bory”” Our local knowledge is not quite sufficient to
fully understand the true inwardness of all the squab-
bling that is going on, but “ Fitzy ** looms largely in it,
and to give him his due, anything that “ Fitzy "’ is
mixed up in is likely to hum ono way or another. The
first general meeting of the Incorporated Fitzgerald
Park Association was held at the “ Shrubberies House,”’
Mardyke, Cork. Ncedlciss to say, what we may ca’ll
“‘tho itzgeraldies,”” were in force at “the Shrubberies.”
Tt appears that Mr. C. Desmond, T.C., is at present,
according to TFitzy,”” “ no more than a cayet.aker of‘tl\e
place—he fired it and aired it, and kept it in repair.”
Tho “ place ” is the house or mansion standing in  the
Shrubberies.”” %

" Who is Mr. C. Desmond, T.C.? We have -been told
that he is nothing less than first cousin of a baronet,
and that baronet is Sir Edward Fitzgerald. There 15
a good story told about “ Fitzy”” and Mr. Desmond that
as far as we know has never yet been published. The
story goes that Sir Edward, never having previously keen
out of Ireland, had a fear of travelling when his duties
as Lord Mayor of Cork compelled him to go to England.
Having occasion to go to London on a certain occasion,
be brought his cousin along with him. As soon as the
train had emerged from the tunnel, Mr. Desmond, e
—so the story goes—in order to impress the other oceu-
pants of the compartment with a sense of the importance
of his friend, talked loud, and at every possible oppor-
tunity addressed “Fitzy ' as “ My Lord” or “Sir
Edward.” At length “ Fitzy ”’ turned on Con, and
said, “Now, my fine fellow, I want to tell you that
from this moment until we get back here again on our
way home you are not to be ‘my lording’ or ‘Sir
Edwarding > me, as I wish you to understand that I am
travelling infra dig, and do not want anyone to know
who I am.”

At the meeting last week at “ the Shrubberies,’” Mr.
F. M. D. Morrogh referred to what he called “the In-
ternational feeding matches’’ that went on in connec-
tion with the Exhibition. Mr. Morrogh said:— He
foresaw at the time that they would want money, and
he hoped that what he had said then was now brought
home to those members who voted for those banquets,
for they had nothing at all now to run the grounds."
The phrase “International feeding matches’ got on
the nerves of several of those present, and there was a
regular debate on the matter—it was almost a wond:r
that Mr. Morrogh was not eaten on the spot. One
gentleman having said that he felt very pained by the
remark, declared, “ He had never eaten as much as a
biscuit belonging to the Exhibition, and that was a
very small thing.”” But what is to become of “the
Shrubberies?” That question is evidently even more
than Cork can decide at present.

Dear Sir,—Your Journal appears to be the only one
in Ireland that speaks the truth in matters affecting the
interests of Irishmen. At a recent enquiry before Mr.
Cowen re the sewage of Dublin, the Corporation actually
brought over an English analyst to give evidence on the
chemical composition of the Dublin sewage! No Irish
chemist could, of course, do this, and an English one
must be brought at great expense to the unfortunate
ratepayers. I wrote to the daily Press on the subject;
they published my letter but I hear nothing on the
subject editorially. A few days later the Cork authori-
ties brought over an English medical man to give evi-
dence on the desirability of a site for a Sanitorium near
Cork.  Again no mere Irishman could form any
opinion worth having. Science, more especially sani-
tary science, has in Ireland little encouragement, and
this system of introducing English experts, in no way
more competent, at great expense, is simply an outrage.
I hope you will draw the attention of the public to the
subject in your open-spoken Journal.—Yours sincerely,

it MP._.BURG AND ST. PATRICK'S DAY.
HIS year St. Patrick’s Day falls on a Saturday,
and Mr. Bung and others are playing that fact
for all it is worth as an excuse to avoid falling into
line with the National sentiment for honouring St:
Patrick. His Grace, the Archbishop of Dublin, in the
course. of his Lenten Pastoral, said:—“ It is necessary
to repeat that the licensed houses that are kept open on
St. Patrick’s Day, are, as a rule, of the lower and less
reputable class.”” He made a strong appeal to the
clergy to do their utmost “to secure a general closing
of licensed houses on St. Patrick’s Day.”’ :

Mr. Bung is defiant; what does he care for St.
Patrick’s honour, not to talk of a mere Archbishop’s
advice, when the “ Saturday takings,”” inflated by the
cxistence of a National holiday, are at stake? ~ The
Secretaryship of the Dublin Bungs’ Association
was, curiously enough, at one time filled by ne
less a personage than Mike MacCarthy, “the
Catholic.”” If Mike were Secretary of the Dub-
lin Bungs’ Association now what a letter we
might have expected! No doubt it would be necessary
to send the Dublin Corporation scavengers to pick up
the literary remains of His Grace of Dublin, once Mike
had dealt with his impertinent advice to the owners of
Dublin drink shops.

One Robert Russell now unworthily fills the post upon
which Mike “the Catholic”’ shed lustre for all time,
and a letter from his pen appeared in the papers one
day last week. Mr. Robert Russell, Secretary to the
Dublin Bungs’ Protection Association, treats His Grace
of Dublin to a few pin pricks; had his predecessor,
Mike “the Catholic,’”” the job in hand, we feel sure he
would have thrown aside the pin and taken up the
bludgeon. Mr. Russell says:—“ My Committee do not
claim to have any influence with the owners of the ‘less
reputable houses’ in the city. They rejoice, however,
in the belief that they might be counted on the fingers
of one hand.” Well, His Grace, the Archbishop, in
his Pastoral remarked that the public-houses kept open
on St. Patrick’s Day are as a rule of the lower and less
reputable class. Mr. Robert Russell in his letter states
that “ some four hundred licensed houses in the Dublin
Metropolitan Police arca remained open,’” and yet the
number of ““less raputable houses ’’ in the city number
five at the outside! Now if the Archbishop is right in
his view that as a\rule those of the “some four hun-
dred ”’ are “less reputable houses,’”” what becomes of
Mr. Robert Russell’s assertion that the *“ less reputable’
houses in Dublin are not more than five? The Arch-
bishop had better mind himself. When his view con-
flicts with the view of Mr. Robert Russell, a successor
of Mike MacCarthy, as Secretary to the Dublin Bungs,
His Grace had better hang his head and retire. How
dare, particularly in these days of * democracy,”’ how
dare an Archbishop hold a view that is anathema to a
Secretary to the Bungs! Bung greases the wheels of
Dublin municipal and Trish national progress, so knavss
and archbishops pdig-a-beattac.

is nothing if not a man of sentiment. ‘“The
per';‘ol:?” says bhegsecreta.ry, who sits where M)ke 2
was once ensconoed, “the person who abuses drink the
members of the licensed trade have always regarded as
their worst enemy ; but they do not believe that he can
be made sober by Act of Parliament, or by tho bem':
porary prohibition of the sale of liquor for ouc._dny‘i7
Who can now say that Bung is not a man 0[, sentiment ?
Bung’s secretary goes on :— St. PMA:]C‘( s Day has
come to be recognised as a National Holiday in Ireland
[not for Bung’s Bond Slaves, Lhqugh.——Eu. L.} and it
appears to be a humiliating reflection upon the character
of her people to say that they cannot, like the people
of any other nation, enjoy such a holiday unless the
public-houses are closed.””  We supposo Bung’s Bond
Slaves could not enjoy tho National oliday unless
they worked for sixteen hours on that day! After all,
Bung would command more respect if he came out plain
and unabashed as a money-grabbing sponger on St. Pat-
rick than as “a man of sentiment.”” Who says that
Bung is selfish, and wants to sponge on St. Patrick,
and desires to keep his unfortunate Bond Slaves at work
on what Bung’s Secretary admits is “recognised as
a National Holiday in Ireland?’ Bah! What non-

on the partial ruin of the nation, on the whole ruin of

ling for an hospital to help to mitigate the havoc he has
wrought.  Oh! most wise and charitable Mr. Bung,
Purgatory, to go no further, surely has no terrors for
a philanthropist like you!

We do not see that the “ truly Catholic ” and “truly
national ”’ Freeman is carrying on 2 campaign in favour
of a proper observance of this great Irish Catholic and
national holiday; fairs and markets are being cited as
obstacles in some places. A man said at a mesting in
Castlerea—“ When you require an Act of Parliament to
honour St. Patrick, Irishmen are down to a low pitch.”
Well, let us recognise that Irishmen of the present time
are at a low pitch, and take steps to procure an Act of
Parliament for the compulsory closing of all bungeries
on St. Patrick’s Day. It appears to us that if a Bill

supported by the unanimous, or nearly unanimous,’

s of the members of the Irish Parliamentary Party
were brought before the present Parliament that the
Ministerial Party would facilitate its passing in every
way. It is little use appealing to Bung’s patriotism ; a
good fight should nevertheless be made to induce him
to shut his doors, and allow his Bond Slaves to take
advantage of the National Holiday on the 17th March
next. Next year we trust that an Act of the British
Parliament will shut the doors of the Irish National
public-houses, and render it unnecessary for the Secre-
tary of the Dublin Bungs—a post once filled by Mike
“ the Catholic ’—to write a letter to the papers.

SHOWED last.week () That the vast majority of
| Catholic children in America are educated, not in
State schools, as Mr. Davitt wanted to mislead the

* public into thinking, but in Catholic schools supported

by Catholics to protect the faith of their children; (4)
That whilst those schools make the teaching of religion
an essential element in their daily education, they
sccure, under great disadvantages, practically as good a
secular instruction for the children as do the State
schools ; (¢) That non-Catholics and even secularists are
beginning to see the folly of the sccular system, and
are admitting that a reform is necessary, many of them
at last admitting that the Catholic system is the true
one for even training worthy citizens. Those-Ameri-
Qans, having tried the secular system, have found it to
be a failure; -Mr. Davitt, not having tried it except in
his imagination, recommends it to us as supremely wise.
This very day a distinguished member of Parliament

THE LEADER.

MarcH 10, 1906.

from Australia has told me that many Protestant
membors who voted for the secular school system there
would long to go back upon the past, and restore their
denominational schools which they then freely and
foolishly yielded up.

It will be remembered that some of those Americans
whom I quoted last week trace the awful spread of
divorce to the absence of religious teaching in the State
schools. In one of his letters Mr. Davitt laughed at
such a connection ; but I presume those Americans know
better than he, He asked the ““ Catholic Democrat *’ in
sarcasm if ho was so silly as to think that the doctrine
of divorce was taught to the children in those State
schools. Now, it is not a subject that is likely often to
come up in primary schools. Bub, considering that it
is so rampant in America, it must sometimes come be-
fore the teachers and pupils, and whenever the subject
of divorce comes up for explanation, what else would
those teachers teach? The doctrine of divorce is em-
bodied in the laws of the States in America. Why not
in consistency teach it, then? At any rate, to contra-
dict it would be to make the teachers tell their pupils
that the ethical wisdom of the State is immoral foolish-
ness. He tells us that divorce evil is rampant in Russia
also although they teach religion in the schools there.
But he forgot to tell us what the Russian Church (which
is merely the creature of the State and the slave of the
Czar) teaches on the subject of divorce. I think its
doctrine is that marriage may be dissolved; and what is
that but to teach divorce as part of its religious teach-
ing? Ah! but, says Mr. Davitt, divorce has overrun
France also where “ Catholic schools were 30 to 1 as
against purely secular schools in France until recent
years.””  Unfortunately, also in France, divorce has
run almost as wild even as Mr, Davitt’s statement re-
garding the proportion of Catholics to State schools.
When Mr. Davitt wrote that Catholic school e i the
proportion of 50 to 1 to State schools in Frauce, T
wonder did he even take the trouble to think whether
he was saying what was true or false? He certainly
did not take the trouble to find out. Ias he ever heard
of what the party led by Montalembert and Lacordaire
fought for? Does he know what is the meaning of the
Falloux Law passed, I think, in 18507 I suppose not;
those involved mere Catholic interests good enough for
narrow ultramontane minds like Montalembert, but
not broad enough for the capacious intellect of our social
philosopher of Dalkey.  Well, the Falloux Law ob-
tained, especially for Catholic secondary schools, the
legal right to exist at all. It does not take very much
brains to gather from that what was their position
before. At any rate, even Mr. Davitt’s imagination
could not hold them responsible for the training of the
French people.  Once they were allowed to live and
work, they multiplied fast over France in spite of State
opposition and discouragement. About a dozen years
ago, I remember that one of the official Education Re-
ports in France declared that the State schools were fast
losing the patronage of all those who could afford to
pay for their children in the Catholic schools. It is
well known that even many of those who voted for secu-
lar schools in Parliament privately sent their own chil-
dren to Catholic schools which they decried in public—
some of them have been publicly taunted with it. Well,
what was the remedy which the secularists proposed to
stop the migration of pupils from the State endowed
sccular schools to the unendowed Catholic schools?
Their method was as simple as injustice—they voted a
vast increase in the number of free bourses in the State
schools. They did not think of improving the educa-
tion given, although the neglect of education in those
secular schools was the plain cause of the success of the
Catholic schools. Their brilliant educational idea—one
worthy of secularists—was a system of bribery, some-
thing on the Trinity College system, or the old “Souper”’
idea. They increased the bourses, that is, they bid for
the purchase of pupil patronage at the expense of the
public. .In other words, they showed by their action
that their public school system was a public fraud.
Nevcx:l;h.eless, the numbers attending the Catholic
schools in France went on increasing in spite of bribes
and poverty until the newest brilliant idea of M. Combes.

~ and his fellow-secularists crushed them like a Nero—of
course in the name of liberty of thought and teaching!
They were succeeding and gaining ground on their edu-
cational merits, for they had nothing else to go on—
that was the ‘“ head and front of their offending.”” The
primary schools were, from Napoleon’s time, under the
almost exclusive control of the University—that did not
mean much Catholicity. Ience, when Mr Davitt
wrote that Catholic schools were in the proportion of
50 to 1 to the secular schools, he simply wrote an un-
truth as rank as a Savannah, and as wild as a prairie.
I don’t mean that he knew he was writing an untruth ;
I believe he did not think about it—he simply wrote it ;
that’s all. Still, we must remember that a man is
vesponsible even when he writes only nonsense; for he
owes it to the public whom he assumes to instruct to
iry and write sense.  Hence, if divorce has grown in
France as rank and wild as this statement of M.
Davitt’s, he must forego the consolation of connecting
it with Catholic schools, even by an analogy of argu-
ment against the  Catholic Democrat,”’ whose argu-
ment he elegantly says “is stupid to the last degree of
controversial banality.””  Of course, the condition of
schools either in America or France is not the only
cause of divorce and other social vices in those countries.
But to bring up children in a religious atmosphere, and
train their pliant minds into sound moral instinets is a
very great preventive. Witness the contrast, in
divorce statistics, between Canada and the United
States. Mr. Davitt seems to think that, in order that
secular schools could be blamed for any responsibility
in the spread of divorce and other social vices, those
principles should be inculcated in the schools. No, Mr.
Davitt; all that is necessary is to let human nature
run its own wild way, untrained and untrammelled by
religious and moral discipline.  Leave it to its own
natural passions, and it is sure to find out the vices all
right—and will cotton to them, too. God knows, it is
weak enough even with religious helps; it is weaker
and more wilful still without them. I know that per-
sons have been trained in secular schools who have
turned out good-living men and practical Catholics. I
believe Mr. Davitt himself is an example. But men
who are specially gifted by nature and grace must not
conclude that, because they escaped, everybody else
should equally succeed. A system of education must
be framed for the needs of the public at large, not
according to the temper of special persoms.  Account
must be taken of the weakness of sinners like myself,
and provision made for us, as well as for stirling natures
like that of Mr. Davitt, for instance. Those who care-
fully count for us persons who have escaped in spite of
the conditions of a secular training, never scem to keep
an account of the numbers who have fallen; at least,
they keep charitably silent on the matter; the 8th
Commandment conveniently comes in there.

As I have said so much about the State schools in
TFrance, I will make a few remarks on their efficiency in
‘mere secular instruction, in which Mr. Davitt thinks
‘that sccular schools, simply because they are secular,
.are incomparable. I will not follow Mr. Davitt in
drawing my information from my inner consciousness,
or from gencral hearsay, or from a note made of what
some man told me somewhere, in some country, some

years ago. My evidence is otherwise. 1. There would be"

Allumination enough in this fact—that the pupils who
could pay frequented the Catholic schools by preference ;
those whom the bribe of a free bourse could fetch went
-to the State schools. For example, in one year, 1890,
the godless school in Lyons cost 2,491,032 francs;
the Catholic schools were worked for 150,000 francs;
.and yet the latter had nearly as many children frequent-
ing them as the former had. The conclusion is'that a
befter education was given in them—but see the extra-
vagant contrast in expense. In 1876, the free bourses
in the State Lycées cost the country 1,000,000 francs;
in 1890 thoy rose to 4,500,000 francs—which means
‘that after the extravagant cost of the ordinary working
oi those schools, their known educational deficiency let:
“the pupils be drawn away into tho Catholic schools, and
the public were imposed with the 4,600,000 francs more,

.as a bribe to attract the poorer pupils. And, notwith-.

THE LEADER.

standing all, the number of children in Catholic schools
increased from 400,000 in 1876 to 800,000 in 1890. Would
My. Davitt account for that strange, but simple, fact?
The children gathered to the Catholic schools, where
they had to pay for their education; they turned away
from the secular schools which they would be paid to
attend. 2. I give another proof. In a recent number
of the Revue des deuw Mondes, M. George Goyan gives
a lamentable account of popular education in France
under the sccular system. He is dealing with secular
instruction only. Ile says that ministerial circulars are
sent around daily, ordering, organisinig, patching and
improving; but they arrive, and there they remain a
dead letter. ITe says that parents who keep their chil-
dren from school are subject to penalties, and yet the
attendance at; schools is lessening continuously. He says
that farm work keeps them away in country places, and
they go so irregularly that many leave school without
knowing how to read a primer. THe says that the in-
feriority of teachers is, in part, responsible. The edu-
cational theorists who championed the secular system
were so careful about the quality of the teachers that
they had them exempt from serving in the army, whilst
ecclesiastical students were not exempt. What was the
consequence? Crowds of young men became teachers,
not that they were either capable or cared to teach, but
simply to escape being soldiers.  Another reason he
gives is—the Government used teachers as electioneer-
ing agents, and their promotion depended not on their
efficiency as teachers, but on the amount of virulence
they showed to every Catholic idea and interest. Thus
the competent saw no advantage in being competent ;
the ignorant were promoted above them, more for irre-
ligious virulence than for efficient teaching. That is
how Mr. Davitt’s fine educational theory about chcosing
teachers on purely pedagogical principles works out in
practice in France. And fine theories about education
can be distorted and manipulated in other countries as
well as in France for other purposes than the educa-
tional interests of the children which secularists pre-
tend to guard.  From what T have said, it appears plain
that the popular education which the secularists have
secured for France, even on the score of mere secular
instruction, is simply an expensive fraud passed upon
the people. Salaries are secured for the teachers, but
not teaching for the pupils. Teachers seem to be paid
and promoted, often not as teachers, but as agents of
political parties, or as apostles of anti-Catholic hate.
That is how secularists systematise popular instruction,
and promote it. We will test Mr. Davitt’s accuracy a
little further next week.

THE IRISH PARTY AND BELFAST QUEEN’S
COLLEGE
N the Freeman of Friday last there is an instructive
I little Parliamentary conversation under the head-
ing, “Grants for Queen’s Colleges,”” and on Page 7 of
the same issue there is printed what the London cor-
respcndent somewhat grandiloquently calls-a “se-

quel ”” to said conversation. Let us look into
the matter a little. In reply to a question
by ex-Chief Secretary Long, it was stated

by the Financial Secretary to the Treasury that “a sum
-of £4,500 is being taken in the estimates for 1906-7 for
buildings at Queen’s College, Belfast.”” That is the
,pith of the little conversation. Now for the “sequel.’”
Quoth the London correspondent:—“ As a sequel to
the questions asked yesterday in the House with re-
spect to the Queen’s College, Belfast, Mr. John Red-
mond has given notice that he will address the follow-
ing inquiry to Mr. Bryce on Monday :— Whether the
Government, propose to give any further endowment to
the Queen’s College, Belfast; whether the attitude of
previous Governments has not been that any additional
endowment of the Queen’s College, Belfast, must be
part of the general settlement of the Irish University
Question; what is the reason for departing from this
attitude, and whether, before doing so, thera has been
any consultation with the representatives of Ireland,
~ especially those interested in the question of University
education?
Z, Ri%htly understood, all this is significant of much.
It helps to explain the state of hopeless muddle in
hich we find the Catholic University Question; it
to justify those extremists who hold that an Trish
‘Westminster is a fraud on the Irish people;

THE LEADER. 41

and further, it is one of several signs that under the
stress of a growing public indignation the Irish Party is
at last waking up to a realisation of the necessity for a
swoeping reform of our educational system if the
country is to be saved from utter shipwreck.

Now, Mr. John Redmond’s inquiry to the Chief Sec-
retary is calculated to convey to the hasty reader that
Parliament has never hitherto thought of granting any
‘“ additional endowment ’’ to Queen’s College, Belfast.
But Mr. John Redmond knows right well that this is
not so. He knows, or ought to know, that over and
over again for the past fifteen years Queen’s College,
Belfast, has been granted additional endowments by
the Government—with the connivance of the Irish
Party. He talks nebulously about the “attitude’’ of
previous governments. What about the ““attitude ”’ of
our M.P.’s? Let us see something of the facts. In
the estimates for 1891-2 under the heading, “Queen’s
College, Belfast,”” there appeared this item :—

Improvement of Schools £2,500

In the estimates for 1892-3, there is a heading—*“ New
Works and Alterations, Queen’s College, Belfast, Che-
mical Laboratory.”” The previous provision of £2,500
was apparently not enough, so provision is made for a
further grant, bringing the whole amount up to £4,134.
In the estimates for 1894-5 provision is made for a fur-
ther, a second, set of buildings estimated to cost £2,500.
Everybody knows the uses of that blessed word “ esti-
mated.””  The Parliamentary volume known as ““ Ap-
propriation Account’’ for 1893-1894 shows that the
actual cost of the first set of buildings “ estimated *’ at
£4,134 had run up to £5,129 odd, an excess of about
£1,000. Again, the Appropriation Account for 1894-5
shows that the actual cost of the second set of buildings
“estimated *’ at £2,500 had run up to £3,250. To
put the matter shortly, in the three years 1891-4 the
Government, whatever its ‘ attitude,”” handed over to
Queen’s College, Belfast, for buildings alone the huge
sum of £8,572. And, mark this, ever since '94 the Bel-
fast College has been getting large additional endow-
ments out of Irish public money. One may well ask
now, as his Grace Archbishop Walsh asked in 1896—
what were our Irish members doing all these years?
(See Dr. Walsh’s book, “ The Irish University Ques-
tion,’’ pages 444 et sg.) Do Mr. Redmond and his fol-
lowers read the Parliamentary estimates! Do they
know of what is really going on under their noses?
Have they, up to this hour, ever lifted a finger or
raised a voice to obstruct the granting of additional en-
dowments to Queen’s College, Belfast, whilst the Catho-
lic grievance remains unredressed? Did the Irish Party
know Belfast was getting all this money? If so, what
are they doing in Parliament? Were they ignorant of
all these transactions? If so, again we ask in wonder-
ment, what good are they doing in Parliament, or what
are they there for?

It is late in the day for Mr. John Redmond to make
his protest, but better late than never. Let us hope the
protest will be sincere and effective. Rip Van Winkle
tactics don’t pay these times. The M.P.’s, no more thar
anybody else, cannot fool all the people all the time.
A “capable Irish Party ably led can still wield a great
influence even in face of huge majorities. But it is
leadership we lack on this University Question, both in
the “House’’ and out of it. Hence our lamentable,.
our disastrous, failure to gain our rights. Intelligence,
ocarnestness, self-devotion—all these we possess, but
these qualities avail but little when the sacred fire of

leadership burns low. C.

GOYERNMENT APPOINTMENTS IN BELFAST.

HE “Saved ”’ are extremely well favoured in Bel-
fast, and again and again have they been given

reason to be thankful for the fact that they belong ta
the fortunate minority in Ireland. The most recent
instance was forthcoming quite recontly. 'The Certify-
ing Surgeon under the Factories Act died.  Need-
less to say, he was a member of the Protestant faith. -
Immediately the position became vacant the local
Unionist and Protestant organisations set to work te

‘Secure the appointment of their nominecs, and had the
-satisfaction of attaining their object. The post, which
s worth between £900 and £1,000 a year, was divided
amongst; three Protestants. In fact a position was
-actually created for the third man—this, we suppose,
in order to appease his friends and himself. There were
a number of Catholic medical gentlemen amongst the
applicants, men who stand pre-eminent in the profes-
sion, but as usual they received no consideration what-
ever. The appointments were made by the Home Office
by one of the permanent officials, but Mr. Herbert Glad-
stone could easily have prevented such a glaring injus-
tice being done the Catholic community of Belfast did
he desire to do so. The Local Government department
has the appointing of two medical referees under the
Workmen’s Compensation Act, and they also belong to
the “Saved.” In connection with the Certifying Sur-
geonship one of the gentlemen appointed has succeeded
his father in'the position, and the office was previously
held by his grandfather, thus proving that—despite re-
peated statements to the contrary—the rule of heredity
still prevails to some extent in Government appoint-
ments.

Two years ago Mr. Wm. MacCormack, Collector of
Income Tax, who was a Catholic, died, and though re-
presentations were made to the Government in behalf of
a prominent local Catholic, the vacancy was filled by
two Protestants.  Again, when the Sessional Crown
Solicitorship became vacant through the death of Mr.
Joseph Carr, it was amalgamated with the office of
Crown Solicitor, which, of course, is held by a Protes-
tant, thus precluding a Catholic from having the ghost
of a chance of participating in the emoluments of the
position.

The other day the Irish Parliamentary Party passed
a resolution requesting constituents not to embarrass
their representatives by asking them to seek favour for
them from the Government. This may be all very well
in so far as it concerns the independence of the Party,
but there is another way to look at the matter. ~Why
should the Party not exert themselves to procure for
the Catholics of this country a fair proportion of the
salaried appointments to which they are justly entitled?
Surely the power and influence of the Party could ac-
complish much in this direction, and if once they sue-
ceeded in breaking down the barrier of bigotry, which
has so long kept Catholic Irishmen out of positions
which they are able to fill, they would, at least, have
something to their credit. But let them act openly,
and in public, and not go crawling to ministers in quiet
recesses of the House of Commons.

It has been alleged that Catholics are apt to “ cringe ™
a good deal once they manage to obtain positions under
the Government. This may be true to a very limited ex-
tent, and there is some explanation for it. A Catholic
finds himself in a Government office, surrounded on all
sides by people alien in faith and nationality to him. TTe
is made to feel that hs is “only a Papist,”’ and if he daves
to exert his manhood he invariably suffers for doing so.
He has no one in the office to whom he can look for
sympathy, and it often follows that he succumbs to the
corruption of his surroundings, whereas if he could

‘rely upon the co-operation of a number of his co-reli-
gionists, he would be able to assert himself without
fear of the consequences.

Catholic Irishmen looked with hope to the advent of
the new Government, but if the recent appointments
“in Belfast are to be taken as a criterion of their policy,
T fear we will not be better off under the Liberal regime
-than we were under the Tories.

ULSTER, 1917—AN ANTICIPATION.
(Being some pages translated from the diary of
Seagan Ua Seagoy, journalist).

+¢ AVE you anything to declare?’ The rough

Northern accent woke me from the excellent
sleep into which I had fallen on leaving Dublin in a
-palatial sleeping car of the Great Northern Railway,
and I found myself confronted by the Customs officer
of His Britannic Majesty. We had arrived at Goragh-
wood station where the railway crosses the frontier line
between Ireland and Ulster.

I hastily assured my interlocutor that I was innocent
of contraband, and proceeded to prepare myself for my
entrance into the Northern capital. I was journeying
North charged with the special mission of reporting the
broceedings at the election then pending in the isolated
portion of England known as Ulster, and I felt no
special pleasure in the task. Things had not gone well
with the North. I recollect excellently the intense in-
terest with which Mr. Bryce’s unique experiment in
egislation had been received by students of European
politics in 1907.  The separation of Norway from
Sweden, the disintegration of the Austrian Empire and
the other political surprises which marked the years 1905
and.1906, had caused much excitement in England. The
Liberal Party returned to power by an exceedingly
arge majority, had found themselves confronted by a
United Irish and Labour Party that would not brook
traffic or delay as the price of its vote. It was clear
that the Irish question must be settled, and settled at
oncz, and Mr. Bryce was entrusted with the task of
introducing a new Home Rule Bill.  Seldom has a
solitician had a harder task. Faced on one side by an
uncompromising National demand for the restoration
of Gratban’s Parliament, and on the other by an Orange
minority armed and truculent, he sought a via media <f
an extraordinary nature. It came to nothing less than
the cutting off of Ulster from the Irish body politic.
Ten years had now passed by, and though in Ireland
the experiment had been eminently successful, the
same could not be said of Ulster. Early in 1909 M.
Sloan, M.P., had taken the ficld with a new political
programme which demanded Union with Treland as the
only course likely to ensure a successful future for
Ulster. The Ulster democracy had seen with envious
eyes the growth of Irish prosperity under the protective
tariffis the Irish National Assembly had introduced, and
the decline of their own under the Free Trade of Eng-
land. The new ship-building industry in Cork was rapidly
out-distancing its Northern rival, and the Ulster linen
factors were being terribly injured by German competi-
#tion against which they could not defend themselves. Mr.
Sloan had, therefore, rapidly found adherents, and now
iwhen a General Election, caused by a violent revulsion
in England against Free Trade, was about to commence,
had opened a vigorous campaign in which his battle cry
was “ Union with Ireland.”” In Belfast the contest
raged hottest, and I was hastening North on the night
‘mail to report its progress.

Presently we rushed through the suburbs of Belfast
and drew into the Central Terminus, where I placed my

" belongings on an electric cab, whose driver, to my groat

relief, understood Irish. I was collecting my impedi-
menta for transference to this vehicle when an excited
newshoy passed me, bawling out at the top of his voico
in English— Attempted assassination of the Viceroy—
‘Sensational details.””” I hastily purchased a copy of the

paper, and switching on the light in my cah, proceeded

THE LEADER. 43

to read it as we sped noiselessly towards my hotel.
The organ was of an ultra Orange description, and
sought in violent language to incriminate the Irish
Unionist Party with the attempted assassination of Lord
Winston Churchill the viceroy, by a half-crazy work-
man, whilst Lord Churchill was driving to the Vice-
regal residence at Holywood. - The act was clearly one
of a half-witted enthusiast, and I so advised my paper
by telephoue.

In the morning I sallied forth early to ascertain how
things were going. It was the eve of the poll. Business
seemed to be temporarily paralysed by the intense poli-
tical excitement. On all sides excited groups of business
men, arbisans and labourers stood discussing the situa-
tion. The walls were placarded with a manifesto signed
by those, veteran politicians, the leaders of the Ulster
Reform Association Lord Duuraven and Mr. William
O’Brien. I read it with interest. It appealed to the
people of Ulster to rely on the intelligence and sym
pathy of the English people, and to return memb
pledged to attend a Conference at which all partics
would be represented, and whose deliberations would be
binding on the whole of Ulster.

The election in Belfast was a three cornered fight.
Mr. Sloan and Mr. T. W. Russell stood on behalf of
the Irish Union League, Mr. O’'Brien and a Mr. Dixon
on behalf of the Ulster Reform Association, Sir Wm.
Moore, K.C. and Sir Wm. Goulding on behalf of the
Orange Party.  All through Ulster the fight raged
around the same standards, but here in Belfast the
struggle was being fought out by the leaders themselves.
As I walked down Donegal Place, a carriage and pair
containing Mr. O’Brien and some friends drove rapidly
by amidst a chilly silence. An obliging passer-by in-
formed me he was on his way to address a meeting cf
workmen at Messrs. Harland and Wolff’s. Thither I
followed him. The carriage was drawn up in the large
yard and round it clustered some hundreds of workmen,
whom the dinner-hour had released. It was a pathetic
spectacle. Mr. O’Brien, bent and grey, dressed in a
flowing cloak and soft hat, was speaking. Once or twice
the old oratorical fire flashed out, especially when he re-
ferred to the conspiracy of silence which he aileged had
been maintained against him by hoth the opposing
parties, and compared it to the Freeman’s policy of
twelve years ago. He maintained that had his policy
of a National Convention been adopted then, Ulster
would have by now become assimilated into a happy and
united Ireland.. The attitude of the listening men was
curious. Little emotion was shown, and save for a few
critical remarks interjected by the followers of the
Orange and Irish Union Parties, little sound either of
assent or dissent escaped the crowd. Once when he re-
ferred to the action of Mr. Sweetman, the Irish Minister
for Agriculture, in imposing a tariff on Ulster food
stuffs, something like a howl escaped the Orange section
of his audience. A reference to Chief Justice Healy’s
decision concerning the naturalization of Ulstermen in
Treland raised a vehement cheer from the other side.
It was clear, however, that Mr. O’Brien and his party
would not count as a serious forcs in to-morrow’s
struggle.

Having transmitted my news to Dublin I betook
myself in the evening to a monster meeting which M.
Sloan’s party were holding in the City Hall. The large
building was crammed with people, and it was only with
great difficulty I succeeded in reaching the seats re-
served for the press. The audience beguiled the in-
tervals by singing, amongst other things, to my intense
surprise, a song in Irish. This proceeding roused the
ire of one member of the audience who belonged to the
opposite camp, and he started firing with a revolver at
the oloctric lamps by way of protest. A small panic
ensued, and several women fainted, but he was promptly
removed by the police. Otherwise the crowd secmed
good humoured and unanimous. =~ The appearance of
I\{Icssr& Sloan and Russell on the platform was the occa-
sion of a memorable scene, the whole audience rising
and singing the Trish National Hymn. Mr. Sloan
opened his speech in Irish, and judging by the ready
applause, many present understood him. His oratorical
methods were extremely sensational. - He marched about
the platform waving his arms and gesticulating fiercely,

but every point in his frenzied oration went home. = His
peroration was remarkable. “If,” he said, “ Ulster is
to progress she must march with the nation. She can-
not work out her destiny if she is separated from the
Irish people. The whole is greater than the part—the
interests of the nation greater than that of the province.
I appeal to-night to the people of Ulster on a plain issue.
There can be no half-way house. Those who are not for
Ireland, those who plead for conciliation and compromise
are against her. Ulster is a tributary stream rushing
towards the great river of the Irish nation. You may
dam up that tributary to-day, you may turn its waters
into harmful channels which lead to the quagmires of
selfishness and class interest, but you cannot stay its
course. It will break those barviers, it will burst
through those dams and rush headlong to-morrow to-
wards the mighty river of nationality and the sea of
freedom.”” For many minutes the applause thundered
at the close, and then T. W. Russell stepped forward
amidst a fresh burst of enthusiastic cheering. He had
a few notes in his hand and spoke more quietly than
Mr. Sloan, but as he warmed to his subject and with
deadly accuracy pinned his opponents to the dissecting
table of logic, his whole manner seemed to change, and
he rapped the notes with his glasses and hit the table
before him with his hand, driving home his points whilst
the whole room vibrated with applause. At the con-
clusion of the proceedings the audience rose and sang
the famous song of Davis the 48 pcet, “ A Nation once
again.”’

As I wended my way back to the hotel, groups of
Orangemen returning from their demonstration passed
me shouting and cheering for their candidates and abus-
ing Sloan. Some of them were semi-intoxicated, but
the other side held their peace. They were content to
let their cause stand the clearer issue of the ballot-box:s.

The battle is over. Sloan and Russell have been re-
turned by colossal majorities. All through Ulster the
resuli has been the same. As I write, the triumphant
party is drawing up an ultimatum for presentation to
the new English Government, which, judging by the re-
turns to hand will be Protectionist and Imperial. As
its leaders have advocated a policy of Hlome Rule all
round, they are not likely to offer serious opposition to
the demands of an United Ulster.

Mr. O’Brien has departed for England to appeal to
the good sense of the English people on behalf of his
party and policy. He explains this in a four-column
letter to this morning’s papers. Rumour states that
the Orange Party is erecting barricades in Sandy Row.
The troops are under arms. By a strange irony of
fate the supporters of English rule have entered upon
rebellion, and Ulster clamours for the United Ireland
it so long and so bitterly opposed. edcm.
